title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a young actor called Jake Turner.
audio_script: Interviewer: My guest today is Jake Turner, a young actor who has just finished his first television series. Jake, how did you get the part? || Guest: I went to an open audition with about two hundred people. I nearly didn't go because I had a cold and felt awful. [20]My drama teacher persuaded me to try because she said I had nothing to lose[/20]. I read two short scenes, then they asked me back the next week. || Interviewer: What was different about acting for television? || Guest: In theatre, you perform the whole story in order and you can feel the audience. Television is much slower. [21]The strangest thing was filming scenes from the end of the story before scenes from the beginning[/21]. You have to remember exactly what your character knows at each moment. || Interviewer: Did you enjoy working with experienced actors? || Guest: Yes, although I was nervous at first. I expected them to be serious all the time, but they were friendly. [22]One actor showed me how to relax before a difficult scene by breathing slowly and standing still[/22]. I still use that trick now. || Interviewer: What was the hardest day of filming? || Guest: There was a scene outside in heavy rain. People think actors just say lines, but there is a lot of waiting. [23]We had to repeat the same short conversation many times while our clothes were getting wetter and colder[/23]. By the end I could hardly feel my hands. || Interviewer: Has being on television changed your daily life? || Guest: A little, but not too much. A few people recognise me in cafés, which is nice but also surprising. [24]The biggest change is that I have to organise schoolwork around interviews and travel[/24]. My friends still treat me exactly the same, which I like. || Interviewer: What do you want to do next? || Guest: I'd love to do another series, but I don't want to rush. For now, [25]I'm going back to a local theatre group because performing live will help me improve[/25]. Television is exciting, but theatre teaches you to listen and react quickly. || Interviewer: How did your family react when they watched the series? || Guest: They were excited, but also a bit funny about it. My little sister kept pausing the programme and laughing whenever I looked too serious. My parents were proud, though they reminded me that school still comes first. That helped me stay normal. || Interviewer: What did you learn about yourself during filming? || Guest: I learnt that I can work even when I am tired or nervous. Some days started very early, and there were many adults telling me where to stand, when to move and how to repeat a scene exactly. At first that felt confusing, but gradually I became more patient. I think that will help me in any job, not just acting. || Interviewer: Do you watch your own scenes? || Guest: I do, but it feels strange. I notice tiny things, like my hands moving too much or my voice sounding different. I try not to be too critical, because watching carefully is useful. It shows me what worked and what I need to practise before the next role.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: My guest today is Jake Turner, a young actor who has just finished his first television series. Jake, how did you get the part?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I went to an open audition with about two hundred people. I nearly didn't go because I had a cold and felt awful. My drama teacher persuaded me to try because she said I had nothing to lose. I read two short scenes, then they asked me back the next week.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was different about acting for television?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: In theatre, you perform the whole story in order and you can feel the audience. Television is much slower. The strangest thing was filming scenes from the end of the story before scenes from the beginning. You have to remember exactly what your character knows at each moment.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Did you enjoy working with experienced actors?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, although I was nervous at first. I expected them to be serious all the time, but they were friendly. One actor showed me how to relax before a difficult scene by breathing slowly and standing still. I still use that trick now.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was the hardest day of filming?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: There was a scene outside in heavy rain. People think actors just say lines, but there is a lot of waiting. We had to repeat the same short conversation many times while our clothes were getting wetter and colder. By the end I could hardly feel my hands.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Has being on television changed your daily life?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: A little, but not too much. A few people recognise me in cafés, which is nice but also surprising. The biggest change is that I have to organise schoolwork around interviews and travel. My friends still treat me exactly the same, which I like.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you want to do next?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I'd love to do another series, but I don't want to rush. For now, I'm going back to a local theatre group because performing live will help me improve. Television is exciting, but theatre teaches you to listen and react quickly.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How did your family react when they watched the series?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: They were excited, but also a bit funny about it. My little sister kept pausing the programme and laughing whenever I looked too serious. My parents were proud, though they reminded me that school still comes first. That helped me stay normal.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What did you learn about yourself during filming?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I learnt that I can work even when I am tired or nervous. Some days started very early, and there were many adults telling me where to stand, when to move and how to repeat a scene exactly. At first that felt confusing, but gradually I became more patient. I think that will help me in any job, not just acting.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you watch your own scenes?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I do, but it feels strange. I notice tiny things, like my hands moving too much or my voice sounding different. I try not to be too critical, because watching carefully is useful. It shows me what worked and what I need to practise before the next role.

questions.question: Why did Jake go to the audition?

options.A: His teacher encouraged him to try.
options.B: He had already met the director.
options.C: He wanted to miss a school lesson.

questions.explanation: Jake says his drama teacher persuaded him to try because he had nothing to lose.

questions.question: What did Jake find strange about television work?

options.B: Scenes were filmed in a different order.

questions.explanation: Jake says the strangest thing was filming scenes from the end before scenes from the beginning.

questions.question: What did an experienced actor teach Jake?

options.A: A way to remember lines quickly.
options.C: A better way to move on camera.

questions.explanation: Jake says an actor showed him how to relax by breathing slowly and standing still.

questions.question: Why was the rainy scene difficult?

options.A: Jake forgot part of the conversation.
options.C: They had to repeat it while getting cold and wet.

questions.explanation: Jake says they repeated the scene many times while their clothes got wetter and colder.

questions.question: What has changed most in Jake's daily life?

options.A: He has to plan schoolwork around acting duties.
options.B: Strangers often stop him in the street.
options.C: His friends behave differently towards him.

questions.explanation: Jake says the biggest change is organising schoolwork around interviews and travel.

questions.question: What is Jake going to do next?

options.A: Join a local theatre group again.
options.B: Start work on a second television series.
options.C: Take a break from acting completely.

questions.explanation: Jake says he is going back to a local theatre group.
